hook: You already know how to solve a single differential equation, but real-world systems — circuits with multiple loops, populations of interacting species, coupled oscillators — involve many equations tangled together. Handling them one at a time quickly becomes impossible, and that's exactly where matrix algebra steps in as a superpower. What if the entire structure of a system's long-term behavior could be read off from just a handful of special numbers?
concept: The unifying idea is that a system of linear differential equations can be treated as a single vector equation $\mathbf{x}' = A\mathbf{x}$, so the algebraic properties of the matrix $A$ — especially its eigenvalues and eigenvectors — completely determine the system's solutions.

topic_roadmap.description: You will sharpen your matrix algebra skills and practice computing eigenvalues and eigenvectors, the essential raw materials for everything that follows.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Homogeneous Systems with Distinct Real Eigenvalues
topic_roadmap.description: Apply eigenvalues and eigenvectors to write the general solution of $\mathbf{x}' = A\mathbf{x}$ when $A$ has distinct real eigenvalues, building the cleanest and most intuitive case first.
Master the techniques needed when eigenvalues are complex or repeated, including the use of generalized eigenvectors to handle defective matrices.
Construct the fundamental matrix $\Phi(t)$ and the matrix exponential $e^{At}$, giving you a compact, unified formula that encodes every solution of a linear system.
Extend your solution toolkit to systems with a forcing term $\mathbf{g}(t)$, identifying the structure of the general solution as a homogeneous part plus a particular solution.
Apply the variation of parameters method using the fundamental matrix to find particular solutions for nonhomogeneous systems with virtually any forcing function.
